data.title: 4 Signs The Market Is Overdue For A Correction
data.description:
Despite continued worries about trade tensions, a potential AI bubble forming, sticky inflation, and a faltering job market, the major indexes continue to trade near all-time highs. Equities overall are trading at valuation levels not seen since the last innings of the Internet Boom, and by some measures at a more extreme valuation.

In today's column, I highlight four signs pointing to the markets having once again become overdue for a correction.

1. Margin Debt Is Exploding

One great measure that 'greed' has been fully embraced by market participants is retail margin debt. And individual investors have boosted their margin debt since April of this year by a whopping 39%. Margin debt now stands at a record $1.13 trillion. And leverage can make selloffs much more brutal as margin calls sweep over the market.

One just has to look at what happened to cryptocurrency investors last Friday, when markets had their worst one-day selloff since the first half of April. Bitcoin fell approximately 10% in a day, and Friday was the largest crypto liquidation event in history. As you can see in the chart above, huge increases in margin debt have come before huge market crashes this century.

2. Insiders Are Selling

While the dumb money is increasing their margin debt, the smart money or insiders are increasingly on the sidelines and taking chips off the table. The insider buy/sell ratio is right near the lowest levels since this metric started to be tracked. Jeff Bezos' stake in Amazon (AMZN) just dipped below 10% for the first time, it should be noted. Historically, when insider purchases surge higher, they have indicated great times to buy equities. Obviously, this reading is signaling just the opposite currently.

3. Cracks Developing In The Credit Markets

Some notable cracks have started to develop in the credit markets over the past month. Subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in September with some $2 billion in obligations. Tricolor was once the 7th leading independent used car dealership in the country. Tricolor's bankruptcy left borrowers, banks, and investors facing heavy financial losses. JP Morgan Chase (JPM) took a $170 million charge due to its exposure to Tricolor in its third-quarter results that came out this week.

Soon after Tricolor's march into bankruptcy, auto parts supplier First Brands went into bankruptcy as well, but with obligations exceeding $10 billion. It should be noted that Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), which announced a merger with Comerica Incorporated (CMA) this month, is on the hook with First Brands for some $700 million. UBS has just over $500 million of exposure to this debacle. Both bankruptcy events seem to have contained their share of shenanigans.

On Thursday, new loan issues were disclosed by both Zion Bancorporation (ZION) and Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL), knocking both stocks down more than 10% in trading yesterday. It was the worst day for the KBW Regional Banking Index versus the S&P 500 since March 2023, during the Silicon Valley Bank implosion.

Are these the only cockroaches out there, or will more emerge from the woodwork? Given U.S. corporate bankruptcies are running at a 15-year high, I would wager on the latter.

4. The AI Narrative Falters

Most of the gains in the markets over the past three years have been driven by the AI Narrative, which is the largest technology paradigm shift since the birth of the internet in the mid-1990s. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) have grown so large, both stocks have market caps larger than the annual GDPs of either France or the U.K. These two names also capture nearly 15 cents of every dollar passively being invested in S&P Index funds. A self-reinforcing and virtuous cycle. The opposite would be true, obviously, if fund flows reversed.

However, as I noted in a recent article, there are chinks developing in this narrative that have driven the markets higher. More and more analysts and pundits are starting to question this AI vendor-financing circle.

It is reminiscent of the vendor financing surge from the likes of Cisco Systems (CSCO), Lucent, and Global Crossing that became a big driver of the latter innings of the Internet Boom at the turn of the century.

McKinsey recently projected the global AI data center buildout will require a cumulative $7 trillion in investment by 2030. Will revenues generated by AI ever justify the investments and debt the tech giants are applying to this effort?

I have my doubts. Amazon Web Services (IE, the 'Cloud') was birthed in 2006. This year, this business is delivering revenues just over $120 billion on an annual run rate. Will AI revenues greatly exceed this and ramp up much faster?

Finally, we have the massive amounts of electricity and freshwater needed to operate these huge data centers. Will the electrical grid be able to bring the additional capacity online fast enough for this buildout? Will more and more communities like Tucson rise up and reject these projects because of these demands? How big of a hit will stocks take if/when this AI Narrative falters further? Only the shadow knows as my late father liked to quip.

Given this view of the overall market, my portfolio remains cautiously positioned. Most of my holdings are within covered call positions around the few equities I am finding in this market with reasonable valuations. I offered up three of these stocks earlier this week. The rest of my portfolio is in short-term Treasuries and cash. I will start to deploy some of this dry powder into the market incrementally on the next significant dip -- an event that, for the reasons listed above, seems likely on the near-term horizon.

data.title: Nvidia's Venture Portfolio Is Its Secret Weapon
data.description:
The semiconductor industry is interesting because it is simultaneously one of the most profitable sectors in the world, while also being one of the most cyclical.

Strong boom periods are often followed by periods of contraction, which leads to a lot of volatility within the space. If you’ve invested in semiconductors through a broad-based fund like iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), you may not have noticed this because different parts of the semiconductor value chain have complementing cycles. Semiconductor manufacturing equipment has different lead times and is exposed to different Capex than memory chips, for example.

That said, make no mistake - historically, semiconductor companies have a boom/bust profile, which is one of the core risks with a stock like Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA).

Sure, cloud titans and scrappy upstarts are buying truckloads of GPUs to power next generation AI applications, but what happens when buyers feel like they have enough chips?

As time has gone on, it appears as though the unique nature of AI and the winner-take-most economics mean that we expect to see a strong base level of demand for Nvidia’s cutting edge chips, especially as the company releases new products.

However, the specter of cyclicality remains.

Normally, this would be a significant long-term risk for investors, but the company has taken a path - it has chosen to reinvest windfall profits into the AI venture ecosystem - to the tune of tens of billions of dollars. Not only that, but the company actively supports its investments with preferred access to chips, demand for compute and more.

Effectively, Nvidia is trading it’s cutting edge chips for equity stakes in application layer companies, a strategy that could produce long-term cash flow stability.

Not only that, but given the breadth and depth of Nvidia's venture portfolio, we expect at least a handful of these companies will produce unbelievable, call-option style returns the materially boost the firm's market cap.

While many analysts spend time focused on the AI buildout, in this article we wanted to focus on Nvidia’s venture holdings. We'll dive into the company's top stakes, outline the strategic importance of these investments, and explain why the portfolio acts as a strong bullish driver for the stock.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Before going to deep on the venture portfolio, it’s probably worth discussing Nvidia’s core business, given that this is - currently - the real money maker. We’ve covered this in depth previously, but suffice it to say that the company is firing on all cylinders right now.

Over the last 12 months, the firm produced $165 billion in revenue, and kept $86.6 billion of that as net income. This level of net income makes Nvidia the world’s fifth most profitable company, only behind Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) Apple (AAPL) and Saudi Aramco:

Not only that, but Nvidia enjoys much higher margins then similarly sized players, with 58% operating margins and 52% net margins:

Finally, on the growth front, over the last 12 months Nvidia grew revenues by 71% YoY, lapping the growth of other leading companies - many times over.

Driving the success, as you’re likely aware, is the AI boom, which has been funded by cloud titans like the aforementioned Google and Microsoft, along with other key cloud players like Amazon (AMZN) and Oracle (ORCL).

On the application side, firms like OpenAI have also committed significant capital towards compute in order to drive product improvements. All of this is a virtuous cycle for Nvidia. Currently producing the best-in-class Blackwell chip, the firm is in a strong position to capture almost all of this demand.

But what happens when firms decide they don’t need to keep up the breakneck pace? What happens when incremental value for building new data centers drops, as it eventually will?

As value generation transfers from the base layer chip producers to application layer companies, one would expect chipmakers, including Nvidia, AMD (AMD), and Intel (INTC) to experience reduced margins and a period of industry contraction.

The Portfolio

This is where the Nvidia's venture portfolio comes in.

Over the last few years NVDA has been pouring most of its profits into investments in the AI startup ecosystem. As you can see, a high percentage of the company’s retained earnings have been kept as short and long term investments:

Initially, these investments benefited Nvidia as much as they benefited underlying startups, due to the fact that many of these firms turned around and purchased NVDA GPUs with the money.

Some have criticized this strategy as a circular investment loop, and we understand why some have likened this to the dot-com bubble.

However, a few specific deals aside, we believe that Nvidia has successfully grown revenues while trading chips for equity stakes in businesses in a healthy way. This is an incredibly strategic way to allocate capital from picks and shovels position.

Now, these equity stakes represent significant upside potential for Nvidia going forward.

Here are 3 investments we think are particularly interesting.

First up, we have OpenAI. Arguably in the lead of the AI race with its popular ChatGPT and Sora products, OpenAI's valuation has grown to $500 billion as of its most recent funding round. While Nvidia's $100 billion investment into the company is expected to execute over a number of years, the size of this investment will still likely leave Nvidia with a significant, double digit stake in the company.

Given how quickly OpenAI has grown revenues, it’s possible that this investment will buttress Nvidia's consolidated cash flow. This - of course - is due to OpenAI's high-quality subscription model that keeps cash coming in the door.

In addition to the stake in OpenAI, Nvidia will also own part of the company's investments, which includes a 10% stake in AMD that came as a part of a recent chip deal. NVDA gains solid diversification and cash flow here from a single allocation.

Secondly, Nvidia’s largest public stock holding is CoreWeave (CRWV), an AI cloud company akin to if Amazon Web Services were a standalone firm built strictly to power AI applications.

It’s true that CRWV is significantly smaller than cloud juggernauts like Microsoft, Google and Amazon, but Nvidia has been supporting the company with preferred access to Blackwell chips, which in turn has made its offering much more competitive within a marketplace facing compute shortages.

Not only that, but Nvidia has also built out a compute marketplace called Lepton, where CRWV is a top provider. By doing this, NVDA is essentially supporting the company by funneling demand for compute directly into the ecosystem.

With a contribution view, in Q2 of 2025, AWS produced roughly $10.1 billion in operating income, with an operating margin of roughly 30%, showcasing what a profitable end state CRWV could potentially end up in with over the next 5 to 10 years.

We’re neutral on CoreWeave as a stock right now given ongoing valuation concerns, but over the long-term, we expect Nvidia's stake in the company to bolster and stabilize consolidated cash flow.

Finally, to give some color to the 'call option' side of Nvidia’s investments, recently, the company closed a $500 million investment into AI driving startup Wayve.

This investment builds on Nvidia's participation in Wayve's series C funding round, and should give the company considerable working capital to bring its driving technology to market.

While, there is considerable competition already in the autonomous driving space by big companies, including Tesla (TSLA) and Waymo (which we’ve covered before) it appears as though Wayve is focused on using Nvidia's technology to power it’s Gen 3 platform, which may end up panning out as a success.

We would argue that the payout diagram for Wayve is quite risky, but the upside is also tremendous if the firm manages to capture part of the autonomous driving market.

Aside from these three investments, NVDA has also made considerable investments into other companies across the ecosystem, including public stakes in firms like Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX), Nebius (NBIS) (which we're quite bullish on) and Intel (INTC), alongside private stakes in everything from DataBricks - a mature venture backed data company - to xAI, Elon Musk's OpenAI competitor:

Other notable investments include £2 billion dedicated to the UK's AI ecosystem, a $500 million+ investment into Perplexity AI, and a $600 million investment into Quantinium, a quantum computing startup.

The Portfolio is truly a "who's who" in the world of AI. That way, no matter who profits the most from AI, NVDA appears set to benefit.

Taken together, Nvidia's organic business and investment portfolio present considerable, de-risked upside, especially as value creation moves from the chip layer to platform companies.

Valuation

Then, we come to the valuation. As enticing NVDA's core business and investment portfolio are, if the valuation didn’t make any sense, then we’d be forced onto the sidelines for the time being.

Thankfully, we think NVDA's current valuation and long-term investment profile appear reasonable.

At present, Nvidia is currently trading in a blended price/earnings ratio of roughly 44x, which is above similarly sized peers like Microsoft, Apple and Google.

However, Nvidia also enjoys a much higher historical growth rate than these companies, and analysts expect the firm to grow at roughly 21% per year over the next 5 to 6 years:

Of course, with the growth rate expected to slow down, we’d expect the market to reevaluate the company to a lower multiple, potentially towards the 30x range.

Well, this does present a multiple contraction scenario, the broader investment case here is still strong even at 30x. In between now and the end of the decade, growth rates imply, a 12% annualized return:

Given how expensive the S&P 500 (SP500) looks right now, we expect this will top the market average.

However, another important piece to consider is the company's investment portfolio, which could grow substantially in size and value over the next five years, especially as the AI ecosystem matures.

Some stakes that NVDA has poured hundreds of millions or billions into could be worth multiples of that by the end of the decade, driving additional upside for shareholders on top of the core business.

This could push annualized returns closer to 15 or 20%, especially as OpenAI - in particular - continues to grow.

This informs our bullish rating.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with an investment in Nvidia, including the aforementioned Capex risk, along with catch-up concerns by competitors, including AMD and Intel.

On the GPU cycle frame, it appears as though the company has been considerably de-risked as a result of the broader venture portfolio.

Similarly, competitive risks would normally represent a much more acute threat, but between Nvidia's backdoor stake in AMD through OpenAI, and the company's direct investment into Intel, we’d argue that as the sector grows, so too should Nvidia.

The only risk that we see that Nvidia has not accounted for is broader market valuation risk. Both Nvidia and the S&P 500 both are trading at frothy valuations, and while Nvidia’s multiple is not necessarily unearned, we expect that the stock could be sold off at the first sign of macroeconomic trouble.

You’ll often see this happen - when the market corrects, expensive winners are typically trimmed before "value" laggards are.

Thus, there’s definitely some timing risk associated with an investment in Nvidia. However, we don’t see this as a material long-term threat to the company's organic performance.

Summary

Here’s the bottom line. Right now, some investors may think that Nvidia looks expensive, trading at 44x earnings with considerable Capex cycle risk and competitive threats from companies like AMD and Intel.

However, aside from Nvidia’s core business lines, which continue to pump out profits, the firm's growing venture portfolio should stabilize Nvidia’s long-term value, while providing call-option-like upside on top of what analysts currently expect.

As a result, we remain bullish on shares of Nvidia, even as the stock trades near all-time highs.

Cheers~

data.title: NIKE Isn't Out Of The Woods Yet
data.description:
NIKE, Inc. (NYSE:NKE) remains one of the world's premier brands, particularly in sports, but its stock has completely lost its luster in the past few years. As shown in the chart below, NIKE has seen 52-week losses of about 14%, while the broader market has set record high after record high. It is understandable that they are in a tough position, though. There's fierce competition, weak consumer confidence, and tariff headwinds to deal with. They reported the most recent earnings at the end of September, and today we'll take a look to see if there are signs of a turnaround for the business.

In the below analysis, it is shown that there are signs that an inflection point is in. However, gross margin contraction continues, and weak cash flows have pressured capital returns. In addition, guidance for Q2 is rather soft, making the near-term outlook still undesirable. Despite continued headwinds and risks, the valuation is currently quite elevated, and so I would say NIKE does not present an attractive risk/reward at this point. Therefore, I have decided to initiate a sell rating.

Performance Has Been Pressured

We'll get into their FY2026 Q1 performance in just a second, but before that, I just want to briefly take a look at the company's performance over the past few years. As you can see, in FY2025, NIKE has underperformed in each of these key metrics. Top and bottom line declines are worrying, and the ROIC has weakened considerably. With that in mind, the major gap between the S&P 500's performance and NIKE stock's performance is really quite a rational reaction in my view. Let's now look for some signs of stabilization.

Top Line Declines Pause

As stated earlier, NIKE reported their most recent earnings at the end of September, so just over two weeks ago. For the top line, they generated a total of $11.73 billion in revenues. That represents a growth rate of 1.01%. If you heard this growth rate in isolation, it wouldn't be impressive. But considering that revenues have been declining every quarter since FY2024 Q4, this marks a major shift for the better. Just in the previous quarter, revenues were down 12% YoY, and so it has been a rather remarkable turn of events. This potentially shows that demand is starting to stabilize as the tariff shock to the U.S. consumer may have subsided somewhat. Lastly, NIKE crushed analyst expectations as they beat them by a whopping $730.23 million, and so their top-line results were much better than feared.

Margin Remains Weak

While the positive revenue growth rate is definitely a welcome sign, there are still considerable concerns for their business. In their breakdown of revenues, they reported that NIKE Direct revenues were down 4% YoY to $4.5 billion. These are their sales through their website and their own retail stores. On the other hand, wholesale revenues increased by 7% to $6.8 billion. The problem with this is that Direct sales have higher margins since there is no middleman. The company would prefer to sell through their own channels rather than rely on other retailers. For gross margin, NIKE reported a 320 basis point contraction to 42.2%. This was the result of higher discounts needed for sales and also because of the channel mix. Therefore, despite some top-line improvement, a closer look shows that the business may not be out of the woods yet.

Capital Returns At Risk?

With the massive margin contraction, it is no surprise that the bottom line results were unfavorable. They reported a diluted EPS of $0.49, down 30% YoY. Do note that back in Q4, EPS dropped by 86%, and so there is actually an improvement. In the previous quarter, gross margin contracted by an even more worrying 440 basis points. Anyhow, while the EPS decline in Q1 wasn't what investors wanted to see, cash flow may be even more of a concern.

As you can see above, NIKE generated a total of $222 million in operating cash flows. That is a 44% decline from the year-ago quarter. While this is already potentially worrying, the company increased CAPEX by 72.5% YoY to $207 million. As a result, free cash flows are barely positive and are at their worst level since FY2024 Q1. This potentially makes capital returns to shareholders a bit shaky.

Seemingly as a result of cash flow weakness, NIKE cut their overall capital returns significantly versus the prior year period. They actually increased their total dividend payout by 6% to $591 million, but for share repurchases, there was a major decrease. In Q1, they bought back a total of $126 million in stock, according to Seeking Alpha data, down 89% YoY. That is a stunning change in policy and shows that the cash flow weakness could be making management cautious. As you can see above, Seeking Alpha Quant also seems a bit wary about their dividend safety, as it is only rated as a D-. At this point, I would say that a dividend cut is highly unlikely, as they still have $8.6 billion in cash and equivalents and short-term investments. Therefore, even if FCF weakness persists, NIKE still has room to continue paying out their dividend.

Q2 Guidance

For FY2026 Q2, NIKE is said to be expecting a YoY revenue decline in the low single digits. That is a bit of a disappointment considering that they saw positive growth in their Q1. This clearly shows that while the worst may be over, demand is still on very shaky ground at the moment.

As for gross margin, they are expecting a 300 to 375 basis point contraction after including an expected new 175 basis point tariff headwind. At the midpoint, this contraction will be bigger than in Q1, and so the unfavorable channel mix is likely to continue for the company.

Lastly, NIKE expects that SG&A expense will increase in the high single-digit percentage range. With revenues expected to decline, this increase in expenses signals that corporate efficiency isn't going to be particularly encouraging either. As a whole, I would say this outlook shows that major speed bumps remain on their path to recovery.

Valuation Is Questionable

NIKE has seen a major TTM P/E ratio expansion after a multiyear low in the multiple earlier this year. As you can see, during the middle of 2025, the P/E broke above its 3 year median, and the current reading of 35.16 represents quite a high level relative to the past few years. At this point, there are reasons to believe that this valuation may not be appropriate.

Despite some signs that the company has seen an inflection point, they are far from being a strong performer. Gross margin continues to contract, and the company has had to cut back on share repurchases, which appears to be a sign of low confidence in cash flows. Furthermore, the guidance discussed above is rather soft and indicates that headwinds could persist.

For sure, the TTM P/E ratio has expanded due to a steep drop in earnings, and that potentially makes the valuation seem higher than it is. However, as you can see, NIKE's forward P/E is also rather elevated, and so investors may be a bit too optimistic regarding the prospects of a turnaround in the near future. Lastly, note that Seeking Alpha Quant rates their overall valuation a D- as a sign of considerable overvaluation.

Final Thoughts

As stated at the beginning, NIKE is a world-class brand. In the very long run, I do see the company returning to stronger performance, but now is not a great time to buy in to the stock, in my view. As of now, there is still much uncertainty over whether the turnaround is actually happening. Sure, there are improvements for the top line, but many metrics are still in unnerving positions. If the stock's valuation was cheap, that would help offset these risks. However, as discussed above, NIKE's quite expensive both according to the TTM P/E and the forward P/E. Therefore, even for investors that believe in the long term outlook for the company, there will likely be better buying opportunities down the road. For that reason, the stock is currently a sell in my view.

data.title: Nebius Stock: Why I'm Finally Buying In
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) has had quite the run this year. Shares have more than tripled in 2025 on the back of strong overall demand for AI capacity and a landmark deal with Microsoft (MSFT) to provide said capacity over the course of the next few years. While this may seem like bubble territory, the amount of capital that hyperscalers are committing to these initiatives is very real, and I think Nebius is in a prime position to continue to win those dollars.

In my previous piece on NBIS last month in the wake of the Microsoft deal (and a 40%+ price pop), I argued that this agreement was only the beginning and that the company offers an attractive value proposition for the big players who need third-party data centers in order to meet their capacity goals. I rated it a Buy. The stock has since gained an additional 30% as the AI hype cycle has continued in earnest. That article can be read here.

And now, as of Thursday's close, Nebius has a market cap of over $30 billion. Of course, not all of this was driven by momentum from the Microsoft deal -- OpenAI (OPENAI) has been making the rounds, knighting various players in the space as important partners in the data center wars to come. OpenAI agreed to a major $300 billion deal with Oracle (ORCL), took an implicit stake in AMD in exchange for buying its GPUs, committed to design custom AI chips with Broadcom (AVGO), and procured 10 GW of capacity from Nvidia (NVDA). Among this flurry, OpenAI also expanded its existing agreement with Nebius competitor CoreWeave (CRWV), by up to $6.5 billion for a total of around $22 billion.

Each of these announcements sent the involved stocks, and the rest of the market, into a frenzy. There's been a lot of discussion about the circular nature of some of these agreements and whether these expenditures are funding supply that will eventually serve legitimate demand or if it's just a high-stakes game of musical chairs. Many people probably think they know the answer to that question, but there's really no way to know for sure, and certainly no way to time the consequences with any level of precision.

Therefore, I think the most useful perspective is to approach it from the perspective of who makes money in the meantime, while the train continues down the tracks. The AI boom has ignited lots of talk about "pick and shovel" plays, with the most obvious one being Nvidia on the GPU side and another important one being Micron (MU), which sells the high-performance memory that underpins those GPUs.

What I think we'll see in the years to come, is the continued outperformance of these upstream suppliers in the AI ecosystem as the downstream hyperscalers carry on their arms race and pray for gold at the end of the rainbow. Which brings us back to Nebius.

There's a bit of a misconception that, similar to CoreWeave, Nebius just bought up a bunch of GPUs and rents that capacity out as a business model. However, the company actually offers a much more comprehensive platform that can more accurately be described as a full-stack AI deployment that includes compute, storage, and developer tools that can get a company up and running on machine learning and AI workflows at scale extremely quickly.

Microsoft's procurement deal with Nebius does not just include access to the 100,000 Nvidia GPUs the company has stockpiled, but also to that full suite of tools, which Microsoft reportedly plans to use to develop LLMs and an AI assistant in-house. This is Nebius' edge over similar so-called "neocloud" providers -- this isn't just a pick and shovel play: this is the pick, shovel, haul truck, bulldozers, etc. Microsoft evidently judged Nebius to offer a superior value proposition, considering it declined to pick up a $12 billion option with CoreWeave back in March after previously signing up for $10 billion in capacity.

If this "full stack" business sounds like a sort of nebulous advantage, I think it's important to remember that software can often be the stickiest part of a tech stack. A major factor in Nvidia's current dominance in GPUs is its CUDA software layer, which has been the standard in parallel computing for decades, in part because the company aggressively pushed its adoption by, for example, giving out free and heavily discounted GPUs to researchers and academics in order to entrench its offering among the people who would actually be using the processors to run workloads. Building and keeping a hardware moat is a tough, never-ending battle, but maintaining a software moat is comparatively easy. Humans are creatures of habit after all.

Relating this back to our current discussion, a company like CoreWeave just owns the metal -- switching a workload elsewhere is as simple as updating a connection endpoint. Nebius' proposition has the potential to be a lot stickier as customers use, develop, and become familiar with the platform.

Of course, this does also mean that Nebius is leaning into the parts of the AI pipeline that might come up empty in the event that demand fails to materialize on the scale that big tech expects, but the company has been smart and measured with its procurement and is significantly less exposed than the true hyperscalers like Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG), who are spending tens of billions of dollars to scale capacity.

And while secular trends are in Nebius' favor, it becomes difficult as an investor to value such a company, considering profitability is barely a blip on the horizon, and projected ARR is hovering around just $1 billion as of Q2 2025. In fact, part of the reason I think NBIS saw such a substantial jump after the Microsoft deal is because it allowed a more concrete evaluation of the company's prospects going forward.

I think it's helpful in these scenarios to follow capex and capacity:

The ~$500 million in quarterly capex is in line with previous guidance for $2 billion for FY2025, and the company expects capacity to explode to more than 1 GW by year-end 2026, up from just 220 MW by year-end 2025. This indicates a company that is anticipating a sharp increase in demand in a short period of time, with Microsoft being just the first of many customers. While there were no official wattage numbers released, Nebius plans to use its Vineland, New Jersey, data center, which is expected to have a max capacity of 300 MW, to service the Microsoft contract, leaving plenty of capacity for additional deals with customers, large and small.

Investor Takeaway

All that said, putting a value on the stock remains difficult. From a capacity standpoint, I think there's evidently more room to secure customers, and if the wattage translates to dollars in a similar ratio as it did in the Microsoft deal, the company's revenues could scale quite quickly. So if it's just a question of securing customers, Nebius looks to be ahead of the pack with its all-inclusive AI platform from hardware to software to security.

While we might see the stock ebb and flow at the whims of the broader AI cohort and as additional customer wins make and then fade from the news cycle, NBIS looks poised for multiple years of strong growth ahead, driven by capacity expansion and a best-in-class value proposition. While I was considering an upgrade to Strong Buy, the current valuation and run-up have me just the slightest bit hesitant, so I am reiterating a Buy and will likely pick up some shares for a small starting position in the days ahead.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: SL Green Realty: Stock Price Ignores Progress
data.description:
SL Green Realty (NYSE:SLG) reported third quarter earnings that were more than satisfactory. But the stock price appears to be concentrating upon other things. The last article noted that FFO was raised after a solid second quarter and the stock price barely noticed. That should change as this is a solid growth situation that has far outperformed the office REIT industry. Because of the status of New York City as an important center for several major industries, that outperformance should continue.

SL Green Realty Stock Price

Long-time readers will remember the market's qualms about SL Green management carrying out the fiscal year 2023 and 2024 goals set. After all, the market was sure there would be a real estate recession (or worse). SLG stock price hit rock bottom on those fears as shown below:

Back in 2023 when management stated time and time again that the way the budget and guidance were to be executed depended upon a realistic price. But Mr. Market acted as though there was no realistic price. Clearly in 2023, the market started off with a completely different idea.

As management sold properties just as they stated they would, the stock price responded accordingly. Evidently, unlike the market fears, the price was right depending upon the subject, and the budget was largely accomplished in those fiscal years to the surprise of the market.

Yet here we are again. Things like threats of tariffs and trade wars are dominating the news and evidently the company prospects in the eyes of the market. So, the stock price response to another decent quarter was "yawn" because obviously there is trouble ahead.

But the key idea here is that New York City is a prime location, and this company has properties in some of the best parts of the city. Those properties are always in demand regardless of the situation. The fact that this company makes a fair amount of money by refurbishing buildings in prime locations is "icing on the cake". Anyone who knows a thing about real estate knows that success is due to "location, location, and location". This company has practiced that for a very long-time and Mr. Market still gets distracted.

Now, on a realistic basis, yes, we could be in for a recession or possibly worse. But the location of the real estate would indicate that this company is usually one of the first to bounce back from such issues. It is very unlikely to be materially different this time around as prime locations do not change all that dramatically over time.

Furthermore, this company is in buying mode as the latest quarterly report indicates. It may take a couple of years to investors to see the results of these purchases. But the track record here of success has long been established. You have experts on the subject of New York commercial real estate in buying mode. Unless you think that they have all of a sudden lost their minds, it is probably not a bad idea to consider following their lead when it comes to buying prime New York City real estate. I have yet to find anyone that knows the market better than this crew.

Hedging The Bet

The company also is servicing properties with a fund that was set up about a year or so ago. The special servicing division grew by roughly $1.6 billion in assets serviced with even more designations should those properties end up needing something like this.

This is really the other side of the equation. The big news before was prime locations and how upgrading and in general running things correctly resulted in decent results.

This division is about taking over properties that ran into trouble and therefore need a professional to manage things until a situation is resolved. But this also gives the company an insight into potential deals that can be had.

Taking over a troubled situation and turning it around is very similar to what this company has done with upgrading properties in prime locations. Chances are that if the company wants to own any of these properties, then those properties can be had at likely bargain prices. An investor can just bet that the company balance sheet can handle whatever deal floats by.

At the same time, servicing of any kind is an "asset lite" strategy. Not much if any cash is needed for servicing purposes. Meanwhile the servicing generates a decent cash flow and possibly some fee income as well. Down the road, this could materially increase company profitability.

Manhattan

The same-store office occupancy took a turn in the direction of guidance for year-end. What investors need to keep in mind is that this is a relatively small player compared to the prices of the real estate that the company holds.

Therefore, quarterly results can be "all over the place" because location variances account for much of that "all over the place". It does not mean that anything is materially incorrect.

SL Green Q3 Earnings

The key idea here is that the debt ratio has remained stable this fiscal year. When the company was in selling mode, the debt ratio did briefly decline which obviously had Mr. Market concerned. Since then, some major rehab projects have completed with leasing well underway.

What has to be kept in mind is that this is a relatively small player with big projects. Right now, those projects are near the bottom of activity levels compared to the recent past. However, the recent buying activity could portend a change in those activity levels in the near future.

It honestly makes sense to buy when there is a lot of market fears. Rehabbing at such a time likely results in low rehab costs to bring something to market during a likely cyclical recovery. Even if the timing is not perfect, the prime location and success of past projects likely signal an outperformance once leasing begins.

Manhattan

Shown below is the important Manhattan market statistics:

Keep in mind that the quarterly results can vary simply based upon the locations that were leased. Therefore, it could take some time to tell from the results if the market is tightening. To a certain extent the investor is dependent upon management representations of market conditions because quarterly results vary so much.

Summary

The company now appears to be in a net buying mode strategy. For a while, it was all about deleveraging and selling properties. That appears to be a less important strategy for the time being. Real estate goes through these cycles all the time. So, what is going on now is to be expected.

Meanwhile, the stock market is worried about events beyond the control of really any management. But New York City is one of the most resilient places to do business when it comes to real estate. In this case, the office buildings are in a prime location that is part of that resilient performance. This company has long outperformed the office REIT industry by a "country mile" and likely then some.

That makes this a continuing strong buy idea. The whole market has got the office REIT industry "in the doghouse". To a certain extent, this stock is suffering from that classification. I would therefore consider both buying and holding until it is clear to the market that the boom times have returned.

In the meantime, the current yield is approaching the lower end of "high yield" territory. Now any REIT has to be regarded as a variable entity that pays dividends based upon current business conditions. But the location seems to imply that the current dividend will at least hold into the new fiscal year. It may in fact, increase. Based upon the current growth plans of management, this dividend should definitely trend higher over time even if a recession is foremost in the mind of Mr. Market.

Risks

The threats of trade wars and tariffs is bringing about enough uncertainty to possibly dampen real estate demand. In this case, that dampening demand has been at least partially offset by the superior location of the company's properties. Whether that can continue or if the uncertainty brings about a dominating recession or worse, is anyone's guess. My own guess is that if the worst happens, it will not last long. But there is no assurance that is an accurate description of the future.

The purchase of any property can fail to meet the expectations of management and result in a loss that sets back the future of the company. Given the record here, this is unlikely.

A joint venture partner that manages the joint venture can prove to be inept which causes issues with profitability and property values when it happens.

data.title: Independent Bank: Merger Complete, Onward And Upward
data.description:
In this column, we pick back up our regional bank Q3 earnings coverage with the just-reported earnings out of Independent Bank Corp. (NASDAQ:INDB). Independent Bank is the holding company for Rockland Trust, a banking system in Massachusetts. Rockland Trust had about 100 branches, so it's a rather small banking network. Then, in July, Independent Bank completed its acquisition of Enterprise Bank, which resulted in the addition of twenty-seven branch locations in northern Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire. This added about $3.9 billion in loans and $4.4 billion in deposits into the fold. Overall, we continue to see stable growth. There is no sign of credit problems here. We monitor many regional banks to get a pulse-check on local economies. The quarter was strong overall.

Independent Bank Q3 Headline Results

Revenues were up 39% from last year, hitting $234 million, but of course that was mostly on the back of the merger. But, there was also a net interest margin improvement. Margins were up 25 basis points to 3.62%. From this quarter on, we will be able to analyze sequential changes now that Enterprise is fully merged. There will be ongoing merger costs for a few quarters, of course. Considering operating expenses and credit loss provisions, overall net income was $34.3 million, or $0.69 per share. But the GAAP figure here was hit by merger expenses. On an adjusted basis that backs out merger-related costs, EPS was $1.55. The revenue was about in line, and the EPS was above consensus expectations by $0.02. So, a decent result. Why did the bank miss expectations?

Independent Bank's Loans And Deposits

Loan and deposit growth trends are key for regional banks. Obviously, the merger brought in extra loans and deposits, but there was also organic growth. Independent Bank Corp. saw loans jump $4 billion, or 27%. However, management noted strong growth in commercial and industrial loans, but a slight organic decline in commercial real estate. Deposits rose 28%, or $4.4 billion, from the sequential quarter, as a result of the merger, but also strong business deposit growth.

Independent Bank's Asset Quality Takes A Hit

Now, with the pickup of Enterprise, it came along with some nonperforming and delinquent loans. Nothing overly worrisome, but it softened the overall asset quality. At the end of Q3, nonperforming loans were up to $86.6 million. From $56.2 million. This is mostly due to the new loan book being brought in, but the key figure to look at is the ratio of nonperforming loans to total loans, which softened to 0.47%, from 0.39% in the sequential quarter. However, we saw a decline in net charge-offs to $1.8 million, versus $6.5 million in Q2. This was just 0.04% of all loans, versus 0.18%. The allowance for credit losses on total loans increased to $190.5 million from $144.8 million in Q2, mostly on the back of the merger, but represented 1.03% of total loans, up from 1.00% of total loans in Q2.

With some major expenses in the quarter, return metrics took a hit. The return on average assets dipped from the sequential quarter. They came in at 0.55%, a 54 basis point decline from Q2. The return on average equity also dipped. But if we look on an operational basis, return metrics actually improved. On an operating basis, Independent Bank generated a return on average assets and a return on average common equity of 1.23% and 8.63%, respectively, in Q3. This was up from 1.09% and 6.99%, respectively, in Q2.

Looking Ahead

The merger is now complete, and the bank moves forward as a larger institution. Organic growth persists. The yield is pretty respectable here, and this year was the 15th consecutive year for dividend increases for shareholders. Asset quality is in fine shape. As the merger is brought fully in, we expect that quality will improve. Further, return metrics are strong. While we rate the bank a Hold, we note the pullback in shares brings the bank closer to the tangible book value of $46.63. We would be buyers in the $50s if it got there.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate Equities Is A Great Prospect Despite Real Challenges
data.description:
Back in June of this year, I decided to upgrade Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (NYSE:ARE) from a "Buy" to a "Strong Buy." Leading up to that point, the company's share price had plummeted. I did acknowledge at that time that there were certain financial and industry headwinds that the company could be exposed to. But at the end of the day, I viewed it as an attractive prospect with long-term growth potential.

Since then, however, the stock has fallen short of expectations, rising only 7.7%, while the S&P 500 is up 12.4%. The firm continues to face certain challenges, but I remain adamant that this play makes sense. With significant investment likely to be made in the life sciences industry over the next several years, and the company having an attractive niche in that market, I firmly believe that it is one of the most appealing REITs out there today. As such, I have no problem maintaining a "Strong Buy" rating on it.

It is worth noting that my assessment could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, after the market closes on Oct. 27, management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts do anticipate a decline in revenue and profitability. But in all likelihood, that pain is already priced in.

Still Bullish And For Good Reason

At present, the newest fundamental data that we have regarding Alexandria Real Estate Equities covers through the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. For that period, revenue for the company came in at $762 million. That represents a decline from the $766.7 million that the company reported the previous year. This is disappointing at first glance. But it is important to keep in mind that management has sold off some assets as of late. In fact, if we exclude any asset sales that occurred after Jan. 1, 2024, revenue would have actually risen by 5.1% year over year.

Management has been working hard on rightsizing the company's portfolio in light of changing market conditions. In fact, in the first half of the year, the company completed $261 million of asset sales. They also had pending transactions amounting to another $525 million. But on top of this, the company plans to sell off another $1.16 billion worth of assets, which should bring the total value of asset sales this year to a whopping $1.95 billion. This is just the midpoint of expectations. It could be as low as $1.45 billion, but it could also be as high as $2.45 billion. As much as I hate to see the firm sell off so much, I think it is important to note that a lot of what it is selling off this year is not producing any revenue for the business. Between 20% and 30% of all asset sales and sales of partial interests this year will be in the form of land that it has.

Management is using the proceeds from these transactions in a couple of different ways. For instance, in April, the company paid back $600 million of unsecured senior notes that bore an annualized interest rate of 3.45%. And in August of this year, which was after the end of the second quarter, the company repaid a secured construction loan in the amount of $153.5 million that bore an annual interest rate of 7.16%. The company continues to make other investments as well. As an example, during the second quarter of this year, management placed into service development and redevelopment projects amounting to 217,774 square feet. This followed 309,494 square feet placed into service during the first quarter. Total construction spending this year is forecasted to range between $1.45 billion and $2.05 billion. On top of this, the company is targeting acquisitions of up to $500 million.

This kind of recycling of its assets is common in the real estate market. Assets that no longer make sense to own, for one reason or another, are divested of, and new investments are made to boost shareholder value in the long run. And of course, when you have a lot of recycling here, you are also bound to have some volatility not only on the top line, but on the bottom line as well. While net income is not particularly important in the REIT market, I do like to cover it all the same. Year over year for the second quarter, this metric went from $42.9 million to a negative $109.6 million. The largest chunk of this downside came from the $129.6 million of impairments that the company experienced when it came to some of its real estate. That's up from only $30.8 million reported a year earlier.

There are even more important profitability metrics to consider. And these ended up being somewhat mixed. For the second quarter of 2025, operating cash flow came in at $460.3 million. That's up from the $411.8 million reported the previous year. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $407.9 million to $443.1 million. FFO, or funds from operations, expanded from $301.6 million to $328.9 million. But on an adjusted basis, we would get a small decline from $405.5 million to $396.4 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company contracted from $554 million to $543.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of this year compared to the same time last year. Revenue declined, and profitability worsened. In fact, except for adjusted operating cash flow, each profitability metric for the company worsened year over year. But again, this is for the same reasons that I've mentioned already.

When it comes to this year in its entirety, management mentioned that FFO per share will come in during 2025 at between $8.11 and $8.31. At the midpoint, that would give us a reading of about $1.40 billion, which would be down from the $1.43 billion reported for last year. They also expect adjusted FFO per share to be somewhere between $9.16 and $9.36. That would be about $1.58 billion at the midpoint, which would represent a drop from the $1.63 billion reported the previous year. Based on how things are going at the moment, I would also say that adjusted operating cash flow should be somewhere around $1.82 billion, while EBITDA should be about $2.19 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what these estimates and what the historical results for the company mean from a valuation standpoint. For what I would consider to be a high-quality REIT, these are multiples that are quite attractive. However, compared to other similar REITs in the healthcare space, I would classify this prospect as still quite cheap. Keeping in mind that there are significant differences between these other companies and Alexandria Real Estate Equities, you can see in the chart below that it is cheaper, using two of these different trading multiples, than any of the five companies I stacked it up against in this market. I also, in the same chart, compared it to five more traditional office REITs. In this case, it's closer to the middle of the group, with two of the five companies trading cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, while three are cheaper when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach.

Leverage should also be considered here. After all, overextending is a big risk for any player in the real estate market. This is where the company has a decent track record, but not a great one. In the chart below, I compared it to the same 10 companies as I did from a valuation standpoint. Three of the five in the healthcare sector have lower leverage than it does. But when it comes to the traditional office space, only one ended up being lower than it is. This means that the company is decent, but not outstanding.

Long term, I am a big believer in the life sciences space. Certainly, companies are incentivized to invest heavily in this industry. According to consulting firm Deloitte, citing an analysis of EvaluatePharma data, around $300 billion in annual revenue is at risk through the year 2030 because of expiring patents on high-revenue products. There is an opportunity in the GLP-1 market for treating things like obesity and diabetes, with that market opportunity worth an estimated $200 billion. But there are already a handful of big winners in that market, and companies that don't have a foothold in that space will find it difficult to achieve any meaningful traction.

Despite these challenges, there is optimism that the global life sciences industry will continue to grow. According to one source, which is a bit outdated because it came out in January of last year, the global medicine spending market is expected to grow to $2.3 trillion by 2028. This would represent an annualized increase from 2023 of between 5% and 8%. In some markets, like the United States, growth could be even higher. To really capitalize on this, Alexandria Real Estate Equities has taken on an interesting strategy. This includes focusing on what it calls megacampus development projects. Examples, pictured above and below, call for significant square footage, with these initiatives aimed at creating a perfect environment for companies in the life sciences space to optimize their efforts. Some of this work has already been done. But a lot of it is still to come.

Long term, I would say that this approach makes the company well positioned to succeed. But it does not mean that there aren't risks to keep in mind. According to the CBRE, years of investment into the life sciences industry have caused a move higher in vacancy rates in the market. According to that organization, in the third quarter of 2024, the vacancy rate in the three largest markets, which would be Boston, San Francisco, and San Diego, came in at a whopping 22%. That compares to the 5% average that was seen over the years leading up to that point. The fact of the matter is that significant completions of projects were made during that window of time. And even in other major markets that fall outside these three largest ones, with examples being Philadelphia, Raleigh, Baltimore, Seattle, Los Angeles, and elsewhere, there was a general uptrend over the last couple of years now. After the pandemic, vacancy rates there dropped to under 7%. But by the third quarter of 2024, they had risen to 12%.

This certainly does not bode well for Alexandria Real Estate Equities. In the chart above, you can see how much annualized rent Alexandria Real Estate Equities gets from these major markets. 35% comes from Boston alone. And combined, the big three account for 73% of the business. Another threat, at least in my opinion, is that broader office vacancy rates have increased across some of the major markets. In the chart below, you can see data for Boston, San Francisco, San Diego, Seattle, and New York City. Combined, these five markets account for 83% of the annualized rent that Alexandria Real Estate Equities receives.

You might think that general office vacancy rates would not be terribly relevant to the business. And I would argue that its megacampus strategy certainly should help insulate it to some extent. Having said that, because of high vacancy rates, many owners of office assets are transitioning their properties to cater to the life sciences market. One example can be seen by looking at a special permit awarded to Blackstone and BioMed Realty in May of this year for a five-story office building near MIT. This property offers 118,265 square feet of space. Under the proposed changes, that property will be transitioned to where 60% of its square footage will be dedicated to laboratory space, while the remaining 40% will involve office spaces. Difficult market conditions in the office industry could cause additional conversions, though, with the recent weakness that we have seen in this space, it is likely that this risk is subsiding.

Even though I am bullish about the company, I will not pretend as though the situation cannot change as new data comes in. As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on Oct. 27. Leading up to that point, analysts anticipate revenue of $754.3 million. That would represent a decline from the $791.6 million reported the previous year and would likely be attributable to the aforementioned changes to the firm's portfolio.

They also expect profitability to worsen, with both earnings per share and adjusted FFO per share likely to decline. In the table above, you can see what impact this would have from a profitability standpoint for the company, with net profits and cash flows likely to decline. The table also shows other cash flow metrics for the third quarter of 2024. A drop in the key areas that I mentioned already would almost certainly have a negative impact on these other metrics as well.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe in the long-term outlook for Alexandria Real Estate Equities. But I'm not shocked to see that it has been a bumpy ride as of late. Some of this has been self-imposed, as management has purposely sold off certain assets to recycle into assets that make more sense for it to own. But in addition to this, there are risks involved in this industry more broadly. Eventually, these issues will work themselves out. And because of its focus on this particular niche, I would say it is well positioned, and shares are attractively priced enough to justify a very bullish outlook.

data.title: Truist Financial Q3 Results: Overall, A Solid Quarter, Reaffirming My Investment Thesis
data.description:
Introduction

Major regional bank Truist Financial Corporation (NYSE:TFC) reported its results for the third quarter of 2025 today. I have held a small position in Truist shares in my broadly diversified portfolio since March 2023, alongside shares in U.S. Bancorp (USB) – on which I gave a brief analysis of the third-quarter results yesterday.

I last covered TFC stock in mid-April after the broader market took a dive after President Trump's "Liberation Day" announcements, and upgraded the stock to Buy. In this update, I'll take a look at Truist's third-quarter results and examine whether management's efforts to streamline operations continue to yield improvements, as well as how the company's asset base has evolved since my last report.

Truist Q3 2025 Earnings: Overall A Solid Quarter, Reaffirming My Investment Thesis

For the third quarter of 2025, Truist Financial reported adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $1.04, 5% above estimates. Revenue of $5.24 billion was virtually in line with consensus and up 1.9% year-over-year, representing significantly weaker growth than competitor U.S. Bancorp, which reported revenue growth of nearly 7%.

But does that mean Truist had a weak quarter compared to U.S. Bancorp?

I 'd argue no, and in this context, it is important to recall that Truist has evolved into a largely conventional bank, especially after completing the sale of its remaining stake in Truist Insurance Holdings in May 2024. In contrast, U.S. Bancorp is diversified across multiple business lines and benefits from a significant contribution from fee-based revenues. These are less cyclical than traditional interest-based revenues and come with a degree of pricing power, depending on the business line and service quality. On a side note, USB's fee-based revenues accounted for 42% of total quarterly revenues and rose 9.5% year-over-year.

At Truist, therefore, the performance of its loan portfolio is much more important, and I found it encouraging that average quarterly loans and leases increased by 2.5% sequentially and 5.7% year-over-year (Figure 1). This is very convincing growth for a major "regional" bank, especially given USB's essentially unchanged loans from a sequential perspective and year-over-year growth of just 1.4%.

Importantly, Truist's strong growth is not being achieved at the expense of unsustainable interest income (measured by net interest margin, NIM) or through excessive risk-taking in lending. Truist's NIM has remained largely unchanged so far in 2025, is slightly above the previous year's level (Figure 2), and is, above all, 26 basis points better than U.S. Bancorp's NIM in the third quarter of 2025. And as shown in Figure 3, the cost of capital remains stable. In particular, stable long-term borrowing costs can be seen as an indicator of balance sheet quality, provided, of course, that underlying market interest rates have not changed materially.

But even when we look at Truist's credit quality metrics, I see no cause for concern. While the nonperforming assets ratio has risen sequentially, this was due to a one-time positive effect in the second quarter and does not signal a general deterioration in credit quality. Year-over-year, the ratio of nonperforming assets to total loans and leases held for investment remained essentially unchanged:

Net charge-offs have improved compared to the previous quarter (not surprising in view of the abovementioned), but also compared to the third quarter of 2024, both in absolute terms and relatively (Figure 5). I find it reassuring that Truist's net charge-off rate remains below the domestic average, which is another sign of continued conservative lending practices. Management expects a modest uptick in Q4, as indicated by the guidance of 0.55% for the full year and given the charge-off ratios for the first and second quarters.

Also on a positive note, Truist's operating profitability continues to move in the right direction, albeit at a slow pace (Figure 6), and the bank still has a long way to go to achieve the synergy targets that were guided for at the time of the merger between BB&T and SunTrust. The efficiency ratio for the third quarter of 2025 was 58.1% or 55.7% after adjusting for (moderate) restructuring costs and amortization of intangible assets. For 2025, Truist expects a positive operating leverage of 100 basis points, which is not spectacular but still a sign of improving fundamentals. Suffice it to say that management expects expenses to remain stable for the rest of the year and, after adjustments (see above), to be approximately $3.0 billion.

Overall, Truist achieved a return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) of 13.6%, largely unchanged from the previous year, thanks primarily to stable net interest margin and expense discipline. By 2027, Truist expects this key profitability metric to improve to 15%, which is certainly encouraging but not spectacular – especially given U.S. Bancorp’s ROTCE of almost 19% in Q3.

Finally, deposits were slightly disappointing in the third quarter (Figure 7), but this should not necessarily be seen as indicative of broader issues, as the outflows were mainly due to two short-term, M&A-related client deposits. As short-term interest rates are likely to continue to decline, pressure on short-term interest-bearing deposits could increase further, although this is more of an industry-wide aspect to note. Conversely, the stabilization of longer-term deposits is likely to have a positive effect, as I expect long-term interest rates to remain higher for longer and M&A activity to be subdued as a result.

From a shareholder return perspective, I believe Truist could afford to return to moderate dividend growth, particularly given its benign loan portfolio and solid capitalization (common equity tier 1, CET1, ratio of 11%). Nevertheless, I welcome the continued emphasis on share repurchases. Compared to the third quarter of 2024, the weighted average number of shares outstanding declined by a meaningful 3.9%.

This means, of course, that EPS growth should be taken with a grain of salt, but I maintain that buying back shares at a book value well below 1 is money well spent (see historical valuation below). According to management - and I see this as a sign of confidence in the capital base and the quality of the loan portfolio - share buybacks are to be stepped up going forward, which should result in a CET1 ratio of 10% by 2027, a decline by one percentage point. In the third quarter, Truist repurchased shares worth $500 million and expects to spend $750 million in the fourth quarter, as it did in the second quarter, bringing the total for 2025 to $2.5 billion. As a reminder, repurchases were much more moderate in 2024, at only $1 billion.

In conclusion, Truist's results for the third quarter of 2025 were reassuring, and I remain confident about holding the stock for the long term. Admittedly, the relatively slow pace of improvement is testing my patience somewhat, but I think the latest results confirm that the foundation is solid, and I maintain that Truist operates in a largely favorable part of the country, which underlines my long-term view.

However, as I explained in my article on U.S. Bancorp, I have turned somewhat cautious in recent weeks. Admittedly, TFC shares are by no means expensive (Figure 8), but given the fairly aggressive recovery of the broader market and the still largely intact macroeconomic uncertainties (to put it politely), I am downgrading the stock to Hold. I would not mind adding to my position in the event of a hypothetical "Liberation Day 2.0" selloff, but I am in no rush to add to bank stocks in general or Truist or U.S. Bancorp in particular in the current environment.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: CoreWeave: 3 Reasons The Core Scientific Acquisition Is Mutually Destructive
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWV) has lost more than 11% of its value since announcing its proposed acquisition of Core Scientific, Inc. (NASDAQ:CORZ) in early July. The decline highlights the burden of post-lockup selling from insiders, compounded by investors’ concerns over the durability of its capital-intensive, debt-ladened business model amid a weaker-than-expected Q2 earnings report disclosed in August.

Meanwhile, Core Scientific shares continue to trade at a negative deal spread of about 33% from the initially proposed transaction value of $9 billion. This potentially signals persistent skepticism amongst its shareholders. With about two weeks left until Core Scientific shareholders vote on the transaction, there have been intensifying debates over whether the merger with CoreWeave would align with the interest of both companies’ investors.

And CoreWeave management has been vocal in debunking the concerns, encouraging Core Scientific shareholders to support the proposed merger in their vote later this month. This includes CoreWeave CEO Michael Intrator’s remarks during a recent Bloomberg News interview, which doubled-down on the anticipated synergies of the deal. Specifically, Intrator aimed to restore investors’ focus on how the merger is expected to enhance CoreWeave’s ability in being a leading turnkey solution provider amid the ongoing AI transformation, while de-risking future expansion.

The prospective synergies of the proposed tie-up were echoed in a subsequent interview on “Mad Money” with host Jim Cramer, where Intrator cited that the transaction will reinforce CoreWeave’s position as the “best solution in the market” against intensifying competition. CoreWeave management’s optimism about the upcoming transaction was further confirmed in a recent press release that targeted “myths” propagated by Two Seas Capital – a key Core Scientific investor – to discourage the proposed merger.

The core rationale for the proposed CoreWeave-Core Scientific merger is similar to what drives CoreWeave’s recent string of AI start-up acquisitions – which is to command sustained trajectory of explosive growth, while realizing cost synergies. Notably, both CoreWeave and Core Scientific have been longstanding partners, with the former being a key customer of the latter’s HPC colocation capacity. However, a deeper dive into both companies’ fundamentals and operating models reveals three key risks that could warrant the upcoming deal mutually destructive:

  1. Severe share dilution;
  2. Ballooning integration costs; and
  3. Heightened leverage and capital intensity.

Taken together, we believe the impending risks of the transaction highlights mismatched valuation expectations, while exposing both companies to additional execution pitfalls and durability concerns over AI spending. This could result in value erosion for both sets of investors, which we believe remains underappreciated.

1. Severe Share Dilution

Under CoreWeave’s currently proposed bid, the company will acquire Core Scientific under an all-stock transaction. Core Scientific shareholders will receive 0.1235 CoreWeave shares per one Core Scientific share; in total, CoreWeave is expected to issue about 54 million new shares for the transaction.

At the time of the announcement on July 7, the proposed deal had an implied value of $9 billion – or about $20 per Core Scientific share. This represented a 66% premium to Core Scientific’s market cap prior to the disclosure of upcoming transaction. Despite the Core Scientific stock’s immediate upsurge following disclosure of the proposed transaction, it swiftly traded back at a negative deal spread, which only started narrowing again in the last two months. Even today, Core Scientific is still trading below the initially proposed equity value, which underscores ongoing skepticism from existing and prospective investors on the transaction, and suggests limited confidence in post-close upside.

Meanwhile, for CoreWeave investors, the transaction would trigger immediate addition of about 54 million shares (0.1235 CoreWeave shares for 441 million diluted Core Scientific shares outstanding based on the acquiree’s implied equity value of $9 billion disclosed on July 7). This represents dilution of approximately 10% based on CoreWeave’s diluted share count of 486,591,000 today.

And Core Scientific investors will be subject to the same dilution risks post-close, in addition to incremental downside stemming from CoreWeave’s recent selloff. Specifically, CoreWeave has lost more than 11% of its value since the proposed acquisition of Core Scientific was announced in early July. At its current price of about $142 per share (October 16 close), this implies a deal value of about $18 per Core Scientific share, compared to about $20 per share at the time the proposed acquisition was initially announced.

The mutual value destruction stemming from dilution pressures is evident. The imminent impact from share dilution is only going to amplify CoreWeave’s eroding valuation premium due to the recent slew of insider selling from executive management and anchor investors, as well as underlying fundamental deterioration. This could lead to post-close liquidation of the newly issued CoreWeave shares from Core Scientific holders as well, worsening risks of downside momentum ahead for both sets of investors.

2. Ballooning Integration Costs

Post-close integration costs could also erode promised synergies from the transaction, and amplify CoreWeave’s near-term losses.

Specifically, CoreWeave management is currently expecting the acquisition of Core Scientific to eliminate more than $10 billion in lease overhead over the next 12 years. This would be realized through $500 million in annualized cost synergies starting 2027. Based on the initial deal value of $9 billion, the transaction implies a multiple of 18x NTM P/E on the projected cost savings. This represents a steep premium to current market benchmarks, especially given CoreWeave’s ballooning losses and uncertain trajectory to profitability.

The extended timing to realizing the estimated cost synergies beginning 2027 also corroborates our concerns about post-close integration costs. This is consistent with ongoing execution risks exhibited in Core Scientific’s standalone operations. Specifically, the company’s currently focused on expanding its HPC colocation facility in Denton, Texas. Upon completion, which is targeted for year-end 2025, the Denton facility will provide 250MW of capacity for CoreWeave. However, Core Scientific has only deployed 8MW as of May, and expected additional deployment of 40MW by the end of Q2 at the time, with no additional updates since.

This accordingly highlights the elevated execution risks facing Core Scientific’s current standalone operations, which is poised to exacerbate CoreWeave’s ballooning losses post-close. The near-term expansion costs at the Denton facility, as well as CoreWeave’s expectations to realize the optionality of adding more than 1GW in gross power capacity post-close is expected to further pressure the inherent capital intensity that the business faces. It’s also expected to increase CoreWeave’s exposure to integration costs stemming from its recent string of other AI start-up acquisitions – including Weights & Biases, Monolith AI, and OpenPipe. Taken together, the risk of persistent margin compression for CoreWeave is evident, which is poised to further concerns over the durability and scalability of its current business model.

We also suspect a risk that CoreWeave’s all-stock acquisition of Core Scientific is part of a broader strategy to reinforce its access to additional external financing. This is corroborated by management’s acknowledgement that the deal is expected to foster “greater financing flexibility” for CoreWeave:

Strategic Benefits of CoreWeave’s Acquisition

Greater Financing Flexibility: CoreWeave can pursue infrastructure financing strategies to finance committed capital expenditures, reducing its overall cost of capital.

Source: “CoreWeave to Acquire Core Scientific” press release, July 7, 2025.

This is likely to worsen durability risks to CoreWeave’s current business model, as it implies intention to take on additional debt. In addition to integration costs, the undertaking also implies exposure to increased financing costs to fund its longer-term operations.

Taken together, it’s evident that there are significant execution hurdles to realizing the cost synergies promised by management. Even if the cost synergies do materialize by 2027, concerns remain over CoreWeave’s impending uptake of additional leverage to sustain its combined operations and power expansion plans post-close. This is poised to add pressure on CoreWeave’s scalability and, inadvertently, its valuation prospects, which both sets of investors will be exposed to.

3. Heightened Leverage and Capital Intensity

Admittedly, the proposed Core Scientific acquisition will be fully funded by the issuance of new CoreWeave shares, limiting the latter’s immediate exposure to additional leverage. Yet the combined entity is poised to raise CoreWeave’s operating cost exposure, as discussed earlier, and eventually increase its capital needs. And this certainly doesn’t bode well with CoreWeave’s already ballooning debt load, which comes with the additional strain of elevated financing costs.

Specifically, CoreWeave has already added $6.4 billion in debt this year. And this number is likely to increase before year-end. The company reported only about $1.2 billion cash ending Q2, yet maintains plans to incur an additional $18 billion in AI spending through 2H25 to meet its reiterated full-year capex guide of $20 billion to $23 billion. As a result, CoreWeave faces an imminent increase to its annual debt costs, which has already increased by four-fold compared to levels incurred in June 2024.

The merger with Core Scientific will add another $1.1 billion in long-term debt to CoreWeave’s balance sheet, and potentially expose it to additional quarterly financing costs of $5 million. This would take CoreWeave’s current debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 5.1x to 5.3x post-close, which is significantly higher than the sub-3x observed at EBITDA-positive peers like IREN (IREN) and sub-1x observed across U.S. hyperscalers.

This set-up only compounds the durability risks facing an increasingly “circular” AI investment ecosystem (discussed here). Specifically, there’s been an emerging trend of investment stakes and reciprocal GPU capacity purchase agreements forged between a closely knitted group of AI companies, including CoreWeave. This has accordingly drawn investors’ concerns to whether sufficient new value is being created to justify the rising caliber of AI spending and address the increasing amount of leverage needed to sustain the cycle.

It also casts a spotlight on concerns over CoreWeave’s scalability, given the imminent requirement for additional debt to cover ballooning losses and survive. This also bodes unfavorably for the hefty premium CoreWeave will be paying for Core Scientific, which management has recently acknowledged:

Driven largely by its partnership with CoreWeave, Core Scientific’s stock price increased by ~ 150% in the 13 months prior to the announcement of the transaction (vs. ~25% for peers over that same period), on top of which CoreWeave is paying a historically high premium of 60%.

Source: CoreWeave 8K filing, October 16, 2025.
