It’s likely the impending CoreWeave-Core Scientific merger will heighten sensitivity over current AI momentum. As recently noted by tech analyst Gene Munster, “If one starts to falter, they all will, and the whole trade could break down.” If demand softens and/or investors’ willingness to provide external funding slows, then CoreWeave’s current cycle of strained cash flows and continued need for further dilution or leverage would spell trouble. This is a risk that current supporters of the impending tie-up are potentially undermining, as the transaction could be mutually destructive by adding incremental stress to CoreWeave’s debt-fuelled business model.

Risks to Our Thesis

On the flip side, the upcoming CoreWeave-Core Scientific merger could potentially reinforce optimism over CoreWeave’s intentions to become a leading turnkey solution provider in the AI era. This could potentially offset near-term concerns over the scalability of CoreWeave’s infrastructure-reliant business model, with penetration into enterprise software opportunities potentially helping with its ballooning losses.

But as discussed in the foregoing analysis, the key immediate concern is the added financial weight of the upcoming transaction. Significantly elevated execution risks persist from now until when management expects the cost synergies to materialize. And CoreWeave’s currently weak fundamentals, yet elevated valuation premium, leave limited margin for error.

Final Thoughts

We believe current interest over CoreWeave’s proposed acquisition of Core Scientific is undermining risks of impending value destruction driven by imminent dilution, cost overruns, and heightened exposure to a “leverage trap.” Although CoreWeave management has been promoting the upcoming transaction as synergistic to its long-term business plan, the looming execution risks are significant and shouldn’t be ignored.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Booking Holdings: Short-Term Risks Create Long-Term Opportunities
data.description:
Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG) has become the unlikely winner in the travel space, outperforming Airbnb, Inc. (NASDAQ:ABNB) by a very wide margin over the past 5-year period. The stock also fared far better than the broader consumer discretionary sector, delivering a 135% return, or almost 19% on an annual basis.

Based on the graph above, one could easily jump to the conclusion that BKNG stock could have become significantly overvalued in recent years, as the saturation of the travel market is quite high. Not only is that not the case for Booking, but the stock has become slightly cheaper in recent years, even as the market-wide multiples are through the roof.

The Share Price In Perspective

Usually, when a stock experiences such strong performance, we also observe a notable increase in its valuation multiples. In such cases, the risk for investors who are buying the stock at elevated multiples is also much higher.

BKNG's recent performance, however, does not fall into that category. In fact, the stock's price/sales multiple is much lower to what it was both 5 and 10 years ago.

Things are not very different when we consider BKNG's free cash flow yield, which has stayed within the 4% to 6% range over the past 10-year period (except for the pandemic lockdowns in 2020 and the two years following that). BKNG is now cheaper than it was 10 years ago - at the end of fiscal year 2015.

Over that period, Booking's revenue increased nearly 3 times (see the indexed revenue graph below), while operating margin remained relatively stable above 30%.

Looking ahead, the company is expected to continue growing at a roughly 10% per annum, which is the same as its main peer - Airbnb.

When we look at the annualized gross travel bookings at the end of each quarter, we can also easily spot periods when BKNG stock is deviating from its business fundamentals. For example, on the graph below, we could see that at the end of Q4 2024, the price/gross travel bookings multiple skyrocketed to nearly 4.5, which foreshadowed lower returns in the coming months.

* Q2 2025 multiple is based on the current share price

As a result, we could see that BKNG's share price is essentially flat on a year-to-date basis, which is what investors should have been prepared for by buying at such high multiples. However, as the share price fell in recent months and gross bookings continued to increase, the stock once again trades at multiples that are in line with historical averages.

When it comes to gross bookings, BKNG has also reported higher year-on-year growth rate in comparison to its peer - Airbnb, despite Booking Holdings being much larger.

Short-Term vs. Long-Term Risks

Before buying a stock like BKNG, investors also need to be aware of both short- and long-term risks for the company. By far the biggest risk in the near term is the state of global travel and the health of consumers across the globe, with the United States and Europe being particularly important.

So far, BKNG management does not appear concerned about the macroeconomic and global geopolitical environment, albeit fears are lingering.

While we recognize there is still elevated uncertainty in the macroeconomic and geopolitical environment, we are pleased to see that global travel demand trends continue to be steady so far in the third quarter

Source: BKNG Q2 2025 Investor Presentation

Consumer confidence in the U.S. continues to deteriorate, and the equity market seems to be pricing in a very rosy scenario about the future that now hangs in the balance.

Economic sentiment in the European Union is not materially different, with the Economic Sentiment Indicator (ESI) remaining quite low.

Nonetheless, bookings continue to grow on the back of higher room night growth by high-single-digits in Europe and low-single-digits in the United States.

Average daily rates, however, fell in the second quarter of 2025 and that's something that investors should keep a close eye on as the year draws to a close.

Gross Bookings growth exceeded the high end of our guidance by one percentage point, driven by about two percentage points of benefit from higher room nights, partially offset by lower accommodation Average Daily Rates (“ADRs”) versus our expectations

Source: BKNG Q2 2025 Investor Presentation

Overall, investors would need to be mindful of the business cycle and its suppression in recent years, as BKNG is a highly cyclical stock and should fall more than the market during a recession.

These risks are far less relevant for long-term investors and well-diversified ones. For them, however, it is BKNG's capital allocation that matters and how the company distributes its cash flow.

During the past 10-year period, BKNG has been growing organically, and as such it did not need to engage in any large M&A deals. As a result, the company was able to spend almost all of its operating cash flow on share buybacks (see the graph below), with net debt issuance covering dividends, capital expenditure, and other items.

Consequently, total shares outstanding declined by 36% since FY 2015, and this has been a massive tailwind for shareholder returns over the past decade.

Looking ahead, however, topline growth is likely to cool off, and BKNG management would most likely rethink its capital allocation strategy. This usually involves large M&A deals and moving away from its traditional business model. We are already seeing the first steps in that regard being made by Airbnb, which is investing significant amounts into new marketplaces, such as experiences and services.

I expect both BKNG and ABNB to significantly step up their efforts to expand into adjacent service segments, both organically and through M&A activities. It remains to be seen which areas of the market would be most lucrative, but I wouldn't rule out any large mergers with mobility providers with the intention to create a travel super app.

Regardless of which direction BKNG takes, this trend would pose significant risks for shareholders, as we often see destruction of shareholder value when a company makes aggressive bets in segments outside its traditional business.

Conclusion

Booking Holdings has proven to be a worthy competitor of the disruptor - Airbnb. Not only that, but BKNG remains reasonably priced and does not exhibit features of extreme investors' optimism. That is why, the stock is a "Buy" despite some notable short-term risks related to global travel and the overall health of the economy. More importantly, long-term investors should pay close attention to Booking Holdings' capital allocation strategy going forward.
